metadata.title: AWS Data Engineer DEA-C01 Practice Questions

questions.question: Which AWS service is a fully managed real-time streaming service that can continuously capture gigabytes of data per second from hundreds of thousands of sources?
options: Amazon Kinesis Data Streams
Amazon SQS
Amazon SNS
AWS Batch
questions.explanation: Amazon Kinesis Data Streams is a real-time data streaming service that can continuously capture gigabytes of data per second from hundreds of thousands of sources such as website clickstreams, IoT telemetry, and application logs.

questions.question: What is the primary difference between Kinesis Data Streams and Kinesis Data Firehose?
options: Data Streams requires you to manage consumers; Firehose automatically delivers data to destinations
Data Streams is serverless; Firehose requires provisioned capacity
Data Streams only supports S3; Firehose supports multiple destinations
Data Streams is for batch processing; Firehose is for real-time processing
questions.explanation: Kinesis Data Streams requires you to build and manage your own consumer applications, while Kinesis Data Firehose is a fully managed delivery service that automatically loads streaming data into destinations like S3, Redshift, OpenSearch, and Splunk.

questions.question: A company needs to ingest clickstream data from its website and deliver it directly to Amazon S3 with minimal operational overhead. Which service should they use?
options: Amazon Kinesis Data Firehose
Amazon Kinesis Data Streams with a custom consumer
Amazon EMR with Spark Streaming
AWS Database Migration Service
questions.explanation: Amazon Kinesis Data Firehose is the simplest way to load streaming data into S3 with near-zero administration. It automatically handles scaling, batching, compression, and encryption without requiring custom consumer code.

questions.question: A data engineer needs to process streaming data where each record must be processed exactly once, and consumers need the ability to replay data. Which configuration should they choose?
options: Kinesis Data Streams with enhanced fan-out consumers
Kinesis Data Firehose with S3 destination
Amazon SQS FIFO queue
Amazon MSK with auto.offset.reset=latest
questions.explanation: Kinesis Data Streams with enhanced fan-out provides dedicated throughput per consumer, supports data replay by reading from any position in the stream, and when combined with the Kinesis Client Library (KCL), can achieve exactly-once processing semantics.

questions.question: What is the maximum retention period for data in Amazon Kinesis Data Streams?
options: 365 days
7 days
30 days
90 days
questions.explanation: Amazon Kinesis Data Streams supports a data retention period from 24 hours (default) up to 365 days. Extended retention beyond 7 days incurs additional charges for long-term retention.

questions.question: A company is migrating from Apache Kafka to a managed AWS service. They need topic-based publish-subscribe, exactly-once semantics, and compatibility with existing Kafka client libraries. Which service should they use?
options: Amazon MSK (Managed Streaming for Apache Kafka)
Amazon Kinesis Data Streams
Amazon Kinesis Data Firehose
Amazon MQ
questions.explanation: Amazon MSK is a fully managed Apache Kafka service that makes it easy to run Kafka workloads on AWS. It is fully compatible with existing Kafka client libraries and supports features like topic-based pub/sub and exactly-once semantics.

questions.question: A streaming pipeline uses Kinesis Data Streams with 4 shards. The data engineer notices that one shard is receiving significantly more traffic than others. What is the MOST likely cause and solution?
options: Hot partition key; redesign the partition key strategy to distribute records more evenly
Insufficient shard count; double the number of shards
Consumer lag; increase the number of consumer instances
Retention period too short; extend the retention window
questions.explanation: A hot shard occurs when the partition key strategy causes uneven data distribution. The solution is to redesign the partition key to ensure more uniform distribution across shards, such as using a random suffix or hash-based approach.

questions.question: An application writes 5,000 records per second to Kinesis Data Streams, with each record averaging 50 KB. What is the minimum number of shards needed to handle this write throughput?
options: 250 shards
5 shards
50 shards
500 shards
questions.explanation: Each Kinesis shard supports 1 MB/s or 1,000 records/s for writes. At 5,000 records/s and 50 KB each, the throughput is 250 MB/s. Since each shard handles 1 MB/s writes, you need 250 shards to handle the data volume. The record count (5,000/s) would only need 5 shards, but the byte throughput is the bottleneck.

questions.question: Which AWS Glue feature automatically discovers and catalogs metadata about data stores?
options: Glue Crawlers
Glue Job Bookmarks
Glue DataBrew
Glue Triggers
questions.explanation: AWS Glue Crawlers connect to data stores, determine the schema and data format, then create metadata tables in the AWS Glue Data Catalog. They automatically discover and register data assets.

questions.question: What is the purpose of AWS Glue Job Bookmarks?
options: To track previously processed data and avoid reprocessing on subsequent job runs
To save frequently used Glue job configurations
To bookmark Glue jobs in the AWS Console for quick access
To create checkpoints for long-running Glue jobs
questions.explanation: AWS Glue Job Bookmarks track data that has already been processed during previous runs of an ETL job. This prevents reprocessing of old data and enables incremental data loading.

questions.question: Which AWS Glue component provides a visual interface for building data transformation pipelines without writing code?
options: AWS Glue DataBrew
AWS Glue Studio
AWS Glue Crawlers
AWS Glue Data Catalog
questions.explanation: AWS Glue DataBrew is a visual data preparation tool that enables users to clean and normalize data without writing code. It provides over 250 built-in transformations and a visual interface for data profiling and transformation.

questions.question: A data engineer is building an ETL job that reads from an S3 data lake partitioned by date. The job runs daily and should only process new data added since the last run. Which Glue feature should they enable?
options: Job Bookmarks
Glue Crawlers with a daily schedule
Push-down predicates
DynamicFrame partitioning
questions.explanation: AWS Glue Job Bookmarks maintain state information about previously processed data, enabling the job to only pick up new data on each run. This is ideal for incremental ETL jobs on partitioned datasets.

questions.question: Which AWS Glue feature allows you to run transformations in Apache Spark without managing the underlying infrastructure?
options: AWS Glue ETL Spark jobs
AWS Glue DataBrew recipes
AWS Glue Crawlers
AWS Glue Schema Registry
questions.explanation: AWS Glue ETL jobs run on a serverless Apache Spark environment. You write transformation logic in Python (PySpark) or Scala, and Glue handles provisioning, scaling, and managing the Spark infrastructure.

questions.question: A Glue ETL job reads data from S3 where some files have inconsistent schemas. Which Glue feature helps handle this schema variation without causing job failures?
options: DynamicFrame with ResolveChoice
Glue Job Bookmarks
Push-down predicates
Glue Data Quality rules
questions.explanation: DynamicFrames in AWS Glue handle schema inconsistencies by using a self-describing format. The ResolveChoice transformation lets you specify how to handle columns that have multiple data types across records.

questions.question: A data engineer is optimizing a Glue ETL job that reads from a large partitioned table in S3. The job only needs data from the last 7 days but is currently scanning all partitions. Which approach will MOST reduce costs and execution time?
options: Use push-down predicates to filter partitions at the Glue Data Catalog level before reading from S3
Add a filter transformation after reading all data into a DynamicFrame
Reduce the number of DPUs allocated to the job
Convert the job from Spark to Python Shell
questions.explanation: Push-down predicates in AWS Glue push partition filtering to the Data Catalog level, so only the S3 partitions matching the predicate are read. This eliminates unnecessary S3 reads and significantly reduces both cost and runtime.

questions.question: A Glue Spark ETL job is failing with out-of-memory errors when processing a 5 TB dataset. The data engineer has already increased DPUs to the maximum. What should they try NEXT?
options: Enable Glue Auto Scaling and repartition the data to avoid skewed partitions
Switch from a Glue Spark job to a Glue Python Shell job
Reduce the number of Glue workers
Increase the Glue job timeout value
questions.explanation: Out-of-memory errors in Spark jobs are often caused by data skew where a few partitions are much larger than others. Repartitioning the data to distribute it evenly across workers, combined with Glue Auto Scaling, allows the cluster to dynamically adjust resources.

questions.question: Which Amazon EMR processing framework is BEST suited for large-scale batch data transformations using distributed computing?
options: Apache Spark
Apache Hive
Presto
Apache HBase
questions.explanation: Apache Spark on EMR is the preferred framework for large-scale batch data transformations. It provides in-memory processing, supports multiple languages (Python, Scala, Java, R), and is significantly faster than MapReduce for most workloads.

questions.question: A data engineer needs to run ad hoc SQL queries on data stored in Amazon S3 without provisioning any infrastructure. Which service should they use?
options: Amazon Athena
Amazon EMR
Amazon Redshift
AWS Glue ETL
questions.explanation: Amazon Athena is a serverless interactive query service that allows you to analyze data in S3 using standard SQL. There is no infrastructure to manage, and you pay only for the queries you run.

questions.question: A company runs nightly ETL jobs on EMR that process terabytes of log data. They want to minimize costs while maintaining performance. Which EMR configuration should they use?
options: On-demand instances for the master node and Spot Instances for core and task nodes
All Spot Instances for the entire cluster
Reserved Instances for all nodes
On-demand instances for all nodes with auto-termination disabled
questions.explanation: The best practice is to use on-demand instances for the master node (to ensure cluster stability) and Spot Instances for core and task nodes (for up to 90% cost savings). Task nodes can tolerate interruptions since they don't store HDFS data.

questions.question: A Spark job on EMR is experiencing slow performance due to excessive shuffling. What transformation technique can reduce shuffle operations?
options: Use broadcast joins for small tables instead of sort-merge joins
Increase the number of Spark executors
Convert all data to CSV format before processing
Disable dynamic allocation
questions.explanation: Broadcast joins send a copy of the smaller table to all executors, eliminating the need for a shuffle operation. This is effective when one table is small enough to fit in memory on each executor (typically under a few hundred MB).

questions.question: A data engineer discovers that a Spark job on EMR has uneven task execution times, with some tasks taking 10x longer than others. What is the MOST likely root cause?
options: Data skew causing uneven partition sizes
Insufficient number of core nodes
S3 throttling on read requests
Master node running out of memory
questions.explanation: Data skew occurs when some partitions contain significantly more data than others, causing certain tasks to take much longer. Solutions include salting join keys, repartitioning data, or using Spark's adaptive query execution (AQE) to handle skew.

questions.question: Which data format provides both columnar storage and efficient compression, making it ideal for analytics workloads on AWS?
options: Apache Parquet
CSV
JSON
XML
questions.explanation: Apache Parquet is a columnar storage format that provides efficient compression and encoding schemes. It is the preferred format for analytics on AWS because services like Athena, Redshift Spectrum, and EMR can skip irrelevant columns, reducing I/O and cost.

questions.question: What is hive-style partitioning in the context of S3 data lakes?
options: Organizing data into directory paths using key=value pairs like year=2026/month=04/
Using Apache Hive as the query engine for S3 data
Splitting large files into equal-sized chunks in S3
Creating separate S3 buckets for each data partition
questions.explanation: Hive-style partitioning organizes data in S3 using directory paths with key=value naming conventions (e.g., s3://bucket/table/year=2026/month=04/). This enables partition pruning in Athena, Glue, and Redshift Spectrum, reducing the amount of data scanned.

questions.question: A data lake stores IoT sensor data partitioned by date. Queries typically filter on both date and device_id. The current partition scheme (date only) causes full partition scans. How should the data engineer improve query performance?
options: Add device_id as a second level partition key (year/month/day/device_id=xxx/)
Remove partitioning and rely on file-level metadata
Switch from Parquet to CSV format
Create a separate S3 bucket for each device
questions.explanation: Adding device_id as a secondary partition key creates a more granular directory structure. Queries filtering on both date and device_id will only scan the relevant sub-partitions, significantly reducing data scanned and improving query performance.

questions.question: A data engineer is choosing between Parquet and ORC formats for a data lake used primarily with Amazon Athena. Which statement is correct?
options: Parquet is the recommended default for Athena and integrates well with the broader AWS ecosystem
ORC always provides better compression than Parquet
Parquet does not support nested data types
ORC is required when using Glue Crawlers
questions.explanation: While both Parquet and ORC are columnar formats, Parquet is the recommended default for Athena and has broader ecosystem support across AWS services including Glue, EMR, and Redshift Spectrum.

questions.question: A data lake receives millions of small files daily (each under 1 MB). Athena queries on this data are extremely slow. What is the BEST approach to fix this?
options: Use a Glue ETL job to compact small files into larger Parquet files (128 MB-512 MB each)
Increase the number of Athena query concurrency limits
Switch from Parquet to JSON format
Add more partitions to the table
questions.explanation: The 'small files problem' is a common performance issue. Each small file requires a separate S3 GET request and task scheduling overhead. Compacting files into larger Parquet files (ideally 128 MB to 512 MB) dramatically reduces overhead and improves query performance.

questions.question: A company needs to replicate data from an on-premises MySQL database to Amazon S3 in near real-time for analytics. Which service is BEST suited for this?
options: AWS Database Migration Service (DMS) with change data capture (CDC)
AWS DataSync
AWS Transfer Family
Amazon Kinesis Data Firehose
questions.explanation: AWS DMS with CDC (change data capture) can continuously replicate changes from the source MySQL database to S3 in near real-time. DMS handles the initial full load and then captures ongoing changes using the database's transaction log.

questions.question: An MSK cluster is receiving data at 500 MB/s across 20 topics. A consumer group is falling behind. Which action will MOST effectively increase consumer throughput?
options: Increase the number of partitions per topic and add more consumers to the consumer group
Increase the MSK broker instance size
Reduce the retention period on the topics
Enable MSK Serverless mode
questions.explanation: In Kafka/MSK, consumer parallelism is limited by the number of partitions since each partition can only be consumed by one consumer per group. Increasing partitions and adding consumers allows more parallel consumption, directly increasing throughput.

questions.question: Which AWS Glue feature validates data quality by allowing you to define rules like 'ColumnValues "age" between 0 and 150' during ETL execution?
options: AWS Glue Data Quality
AWS Glue Job Bookmarks
AWS Glue Schema Registry
AWS Glue DataBrew profiling
questions.explanation: AWS Glue Data Quality allows you to define DQDL (Data Quality Definition Language) rules that are evaluated during ETL job execution. You can set rules for completeness, uniqueness, ranges, and custom expressions, and choose to fail the job or route bad records.

questions.question: A data engineer needs to process a 10 TB dataset with complex multi-step transformations including joins, aggregations, and window functions. The pipeline must be cost-effective and complete within 2 hours. Which approach is BEST?
options: Amazon EMR with Spark on a transient cluster using Spot Instances
AWS Glue Python Shell job
Amazon Athena CTAS (Create Table As Select) query
AWS Lambda with S3 event triggers
questions.explanation: For complex transformations on a 10 TB dataset, EMR with Spark provides the best combination of performance and cost. A transient cluster that terminates after the job reduces costs, and Spot Instances provide up to 90% savings. Glue Python Shell lacks distributed processing, Athena has query size limits, and Lambda has a 15-minute timeout.

questions.question: Which service should a data engineer use to trigger an AWS Lambda function in response to new objects being uploaded to an S3 bucket?
options: S3 Event Notifications
Amazon Kinesis Data Streams
AWS Step Functions
Amazon EventBridge Scheduler
questions.explanation: S3 Event Notifications can directly trigger Lambda functions when objects are created, deleted, or modified in an S3 bucket. This is the simplest pattern for event-driven ingestion from S3.

questions.question: A company receives data files via SFTP from external partners. They need to automatically ingest these files into S3 for processing. Which AWS service provides managed SFTP capability?
options: AWS Transfer Family
AWS DataSync
AWS Direct Connect
Amazon Kinesis Data Firehose
questions.explanation: AWS Transfer Family provides fully managed SFTP, FTPS, and FTP endpoints that map directly to S3 (or EFS). External partners can continue using their existing SFTP workflows while data is automatically stored in S3.

questions.question: A Glue ETL job processes data from a JDBC source (RDS PostgreSQL). The job is very slow because it reads the entire table through a single connection. How should the data engineer parallelize the reads?
options: Configure hashfield or hashexpression in the JDBC connection to enable parallel reads across multiple Spark partitions
Increase the DPU count for the Glue job
Enable Glue Job Bookmarks
Switch from a Spark job to a Python Shell job
questions.explanation: By configuring hashfield (or hashexpression) on the JDBC connection, Glue distributes the table read across multiple Spark partitions based on a hash of the specified column. This parallelizes reads across multiple JDBC connections, significantly improving throughput.

questions.question: Which data format is self-describing, supports schema evolution, and is commonly used for streaming data with Kafka/MSK?
options: Apache Avro
Apache Parquet
CSV
Apache ORC
questions.explanation: Apache Avro is a row-based format with a compact binary encoding. It embeds the schema within the data, supports schema evolution (adding/removing fields), and is the default serialization format for Apache Kafka, making it ideal for streaming use cases.

questions.question: What is Amazon Kinesis Data Analytics primarily used for?
options: Running real-time SQL queries or Apache Flink applications on streaming data
Storing streaming data in S3
Managing Kafka clusters
Orchestrating batch ETL jobs
questions.explanation: Amazon Kinesis Data Analytics allows you to process and analyze streaming data in real-time using SQL or Apache Flink. It can read from Kinesis Data Streams or MSK and write results to various destinations.

questions.question: Which AWS Glue component provides a visual drag-and-drop interface for authoring, running, and monitoring ETL jobs?
options: AWS Glue Studio
AWS Glue DataBrew
AWS Glue Data Catalog
AWS Glue Schema Registry
questions.explanation: AWS Glue Studio provides a visual interface for creating, running, and monitoring Glue ETL jobs. Users can build data transformation pipelines using a drag-and-drop interface with visual nodes for sources, transforms, and targets.

questions.question: A real-time analytics pipeline uses Kinesis Data Streams. During a traffic spike, the PutRecord API returns ProvisionedThroughputExceededException errors. What should the data engineer do FIRST?
options: Implement exponential backoff and retry logic in the producer, then consider resharding
Immediately double the number of shards
Switch to Kinesis Data Firehose
Increase the data retention period
questions.explanation: The immediate fix is implementing exponential backoff and retry logic in the producer to handle transient throttling. Then evaluate whether resharding (splitting hot shards) is needed for sustained throughput. Doubling all shards is wasteful if only specific shards are hot.

questions.question: A data engineer needs to convert a data lake from JSON to Parquet format while the lake continues to receive new data. The conversion must be atomic — queries should never see partial results. Which approach is BEST?
options: Use Athena CTAS to create new Parquet tables in a separate location, then update Glue Data Catalog pointers atomically
Overwrite JSON files with Parquet files in place
Create a Glue job that deletes JSON files after converting each one
Stop all ingestion, convert all files, then resume ingestion
questions.explanation: Athena CTAS (Create Table As Select) creates new Parquet files in a separate S3 location. Once complete, you atomically update the Glue Data Catalog to point to the new location. This ensures queries always see complete, consistent data.

questions.question: Which EMR feature allows a cluster to automatically add or remove instances based on workload demand?
options: EMR Managed Scaling
EMR Instance Fleets
EMR Step execution
EMR Notebooks
questions.explanation: EMR Managed Scaling automatically adjusts the number of instances in core and task instance groups based on cluster metrics like YARN memory and HDFS utilization. This optimizes both performance and cost.

questions.question: A data engineer needs to ingest data from multiple SaaS applications (Salesforce, ServiceNow, Zendesk) into S3. Which AWS service provides pre-built connectors for this purpose?
options: Amazon AppFlow
AWS Glue Crawlers
Amazon Kinesis Data Firehose
AWS Database Migration Service
questions.explanation: Amazon AppFlow provides pre-built connectors to over 50 SaaS applications including Salesforce, ServiceNow, and Zendesk. It can transfer data on a schedule or in response to events, delivering to S3, Redshift, or other destinations.

questions.question: A data lake table is partitioned by date and currently has 50,000 partitions. Athena queries are slow due to partition metadata overhead. What should the data engineer do?
options: Enable partition projection in Athena to eliminate metadata lookups from the Glue Data Catalog
Delete old partitions to reduce the total count
Increase the Athena query timeout
Switch from Athena to Redshift for all queries
questions.explanation: Partition projection tells Athena how to compute partition values mathematically instead of looking them up in the Glue Data Catalog. For tables with many partitions, this eliminates the metadata retrieval bottleneck and significantly speeds up query planning.

questions.question: Which AWS Glue feature enforces and manages schemas for streaming data from Kinesis or MSK?
options: AWS Glue Schema Registry
AWS Glue Data Catalog
AWS Glue Crawlers
AWS Glue DataBrew
questions.explanation: AWS Glue Schema Registry provides schema management for streaming data. It validates schemas for Kinesis Data Streams and MSK, supports schema versioning, and ensures compatibility between producers and consumers.

questions.question: A company needs to perform real-time anomaly detection on streaming IoT data with sub-second latency. The solution must integrate with Kinesis Data Streams. Which approach is MOST appropriate?
options: Amazon Kinesis Data Analytics with an Apache Flink application using tumbling windows
AWS Lambda triggered by Kinesis with a batch size of 1
Amazon EMR with Spark Structured Streaming reading from Kinesis
AWS Glue Streaming ETL job
questions.explanation: Kinesis Data Analytics with Apache Flink provides sub-second processing with built-in windowing functions (tumbling, sliding, session windows) that are ideal for real-time anomaly detection. Flink's event-time processing and stateful operators make complex stream processing manageable.

questions.question: A data engineer wants to run SQL queries interactively on large datasets in S3 using EMR. Which EMR application is BEST for this use case?
options: Presto (Trino)
Apache Spark
Apache Hive with Tez
Apache HBase
questions.explanation: Presto (now Trino) on EMR is a distributed SQL query engine designed for interactive analytics. It is optimized for low-latency queries on large datasets in S3 and supports standard ANSI SQL, making it ideal for ad-hoc interactive analysis.

questions.question: In AWS Glue, what is a DynamicFrame?
options: A distributed collection of data similar to a Spark DataFrame but with additional schema flexibility
A visual frame in Glue Studio for designing ETL pipelines
A metadata object stored in the Glue Data Catalog
A scheduling mechanism for Glue job triggers
questions.explanation: A DynamicFrame is an AWS Glue extension of Apache Spark DataFrames that provides additional flexibility for ETL. Unlike DataFrames that require a fixed schema, DynamicFrames can handle records with different schemas in the same dataset.

questions.question: Which AWS service is used to migrate data from on-premises storage systems to AWS over the network at high speed?
options: AWS DataSync
AWS Snowball
AWS Transfer Family
Amazon Kinesis Data Firehose
questions.explanation: AWS DataSync is an online data transfer service that automates moving data between on-premises storage and AWS storage services (S3, EFS, FSx). It can fully utilize network bandwidth and provides built-in encryption and data validation.

questions.question: A data engineer discovers that Athena queries against a Parquet-formatted table are not achieving expected column pruning benefits. Queries reading a single column still scan nearly all data. What is the MOST likely cause?
options: The Parquet files were written with a very large row group size, and each row group contains too much data per column chunk
Athena does not support column pruning for Parquet
The table is not registered in the Glue Data Catalog
The Parquet files are compressed with GZIP
questions.explanation: If Parquet files have very large row groups or were written with inefficient settings, the column chunks within each row group can be large. Athena reads entire column chunks, so oversized row groups reduce the effectiveness of column pruning. Properly sized row groups (64-128 MB per file) optimize column pruning.

questions.question: A Spark job on EMR is writing output to S3 but frequently fails with HTTP 503 Slow Down errors. What is the MOST effective solution?
options: Enable the EMRFS S3-optimized committer and configure output to use randomized prefixes
Reduce the number of Spark executors
Switch from S3 to HDFS for output
Increase the Spark driver memory
questions.explanation: S3 503 errors occur when request rates exceed partition throughput limits. The EMRFS S3-optimized committer reduces the number of S3 API calls during writes, and randomized prefixes distribute requests across S3 partitions, avoiding hot spots.

questions.question: Kinesis Data Firehose supports data transformation before delivery. Which service does Firehose invoke for custom transformations?
options: AWS Lambda
AWS Glue ETL
Amazon EMR
AWS Step Functions
questions.explanation: Kinesis Data Firehose can invoke an AWS Lambda function to transform records before delivering them to the destination. The Lambda function receives a batch of records, processes them, and returns the transformed records back to Firehose.

questions.question: A Glue Crawler runs against an S3 path and creates a single table, but the path actually contains data from multiple logical sources. How should the data engineer configure the crawler to create separate tables?
options: Set the crawler to create a table per S3 path and configure separate include paths for each source
Run multiple Glue ETL jobs instead of crawlers
Convert all data to a single format before crawling
Increase the crawler's DPU allocation
questions.explanation: By configuring the crawler with separate include paths for each logical source and setting the table-level grouping behavior, the crawler will create distinct tables in the Data Catalog for each path rather than merging them into one.

questions.question: A data engineer needs to implement a streaming ETL pipeline that reads from MSK, performs stateful aggregations with exactly-once processing guarantees, and writes to S3 in Parquet format. Which solution provides the STRONGEST processing guarantees?
options: Kinesis Data Analytics with Apache Flink using Kafka connector and checkpointing enabled
AWS Glue Streaming ETL with Job Bookmarks
Spark Structured Streaming on EMR with at-least-once semantics
Lambda function triggered by MSK event source mapping
questions.explanation: Apache Flink on Kinesis Data Analytics provides exactly-once processing semantics through its distributed snapshot (checkpointing) mechanism. Combined with the Kafka connector's offset management and Flink's two-phase commit protocol, it provides end-to-end exactly-once guarantees.

questions.question: Which S3 storage class automatically moves objects between access tiers based on changing access patterns?
options: S3 Intelligent-Tiering
S3 Standard
S3 Glacier Deep Archive
S3 One Zone-IA
questions.explanation: S3 Intelligent-Tiering automatically moves objects between frequent access, infrequent access, and archive tiers based on changing access patterns. There are no retrieval fees when data moves between tiers, making it ideal when access patterns are unpredictable.

questions.question: What is the purpose of an S3 Lifecycle Policy?
options: To automatically transition objects between storage classes or expire them based on age
To control who can access objects in an S3 bucket
To replicate objects across AWS regions
To encrypt objects at rest
questions.explanation: S3 Lifecycle Policies automate the transition of objects between storage classes (e.g., Standard to IA to Glacier) and can delete objects after a specified number of days. This helps optimize storage costs over time.

questions.question: A company stores raw log data in S3 that is accessed frequently for the first 30 days, rarely after that, and never after 1 year. Which lifecycle configuration is MOST cost-effective?
options: S3 Standard for 30 days, transition to S3 Standard-IA, then to S3 Glacier after 90 days, delete after 365 days
Keep everything in S3 Standard indefinitely
Use S3 Glacier from day one
Use S3 One Zone-IA from day one
questions.explanation: This lifecycle policy matches the access patterns: S3 Standard for the first 30 days of frequent access, S3 Standard-IA for the infrequent access period, S3 Glacier for long-term archival, and automatic deletion after 1 year.

questions.question: Which Amazon Redshift feature allows you to query data stored in S3 without loading it into Redshift tables?
options: Redshift Spectrum
Redshift Concurrency Scaling
Redshift Materialized Views
Redshift Workload Management
questions.explanation: Redshift Spectrum allows you to run SQL queries directly against exabytes of data in S3 without loading it into Redshift. It uses external tables defined in the Glue Data Catalog and pushes compute down to a dedicated Spectrum layer.

questions.question: What are the three Redshift distribution styles?
options: KEY, ALL, and EVEN
HASH, RANGE, and LIST
ROUND-ROBIN, RANDOM, and SORTED
PRIMARY, SECONDARY, and TERTIARY
questions.explanation: Redshift offers three distribution styles: KEY (distributes rows based on a column value for co-located joins), ALL (copies the entire table to every node for small dimension tables), and EVEN (distributes rows round-robin for uniform distribution).

questions.question: A Redshift data warehouse has a large fact table that is frequently joined with a small dimension table (10,000 rows). Which distribution style should be used for the dimension table?
options: ALL distribution
KEY distribution on the join column
EVEN distribution
AUTO distribution
questions.explanation: The ALL distribution style copies the entire table to every compute node. For small dimension tables, this eliminates the need for data redistribution during joins with large fact tables, significantly improving join performance.

questions.question: Which Redshift feature automatically adds temporary compute capacity to handle spikes in query concurrency?
options: Concurrency Scaling
Elastic Resize
Redshift Spectrum
Workload Management (WLM) queues
questions.explanation: Redshift Concurrency Scaling automatically adds additional cluster capacity when queries are queued due to high concurrency. It provides consistent query performance during peak demand and charges per-second only for the additional capacity used.

questions.question: What is the AWS Glue Data Catalog?
options: A centralized metadata repository that stores table definitions, schemas, and locations for data assets
A data transformation engine that runs ETL jobs
A data visualization tool for creating dashboards
A machine learning model training service
questions.explanation: The AWS Glue Data Catalog is a managed metadata repository compatible with the Apache Hive metastore. It stores table definitions, column schemas, partition information, and data locations, serving as a central catalog for Athena, Redshift Spectrum, EMR, and Glue ETL.

questions.question: A data engineer needs to store a data lake on S3 that supports ACID transactions, time travel queries, and efficient upserts. Which table format should they use?
options: Apache Iceberg
Standard Hive-partitioned Parquet
CSV with Glue Crawlers
JSON with partition projection
questions.explanation: Apache Iceberg is an open table format for data lakes that provides ACID transactions, time travel (querying historical snapshots), schema evolution, and efficient upserts/deletes. It is natively supported by Athena, EMR, and Glue.

questions.question: Which S3 feature should be enabled to protect against accidental deletion of critical data lake objects?
options: S3 Versioning combined with MFA Delete
S3 Intelligent-Tiering
S3 Transfer Acceleration
S3 Select
questions.explanation: S3 Versioning preserves every version of an object, protecting against accidental overwrites and deletions. MFA Delete adds an additional layer by requiring multi-factor authentication to permanently delete object versions or change versioning state.

questions.question: A Redshift cluster experiences slow query performance on a 2 billion row fact table. The most common query pattern filters on order_date and joins on customer_id. Which combination of sort key and distribution key is OPTIMAL?
options: Sort key: order_date (for range-restricted scans), Distribution key: customer_id (to co-locate joins)
Sort key: customer_id, Distribution key: order_date
Sort key: INTERLEAVED on both columns, Distribution: EVEN
No sort key, Distribution: ALL
questions.explanation: Setting order_date as the sort key enables Redshift to skip blocks that don't match date range filters (zone map optimization). Using customer_id as the distribution key co-locates rows that will be joined, minimizing data redistribution during joins.

questions.question: A data engineer wants to maintain data quality by preventing schema changes in an S3-based data lake from breaking downstream consumers. Which service provides schema versioning and compatibility checks for streaming data?
options: AWS Glue Schema Registry
AWS Glue Data Quality
Amazon Macie
AWS Config
questions.explanation: The AWS Glue Schema Registry provides schema versioning and compatibility modes (BACKWARD, FORWARD, FULL) that prevent incompatible schema changes from breaking consumers. It integrates with Kinesis Data Streams and Amazon MSK.

questions.question: A data lake on S3 uses Apache Iceberg tables. The data engineer notices that query performance degrades over time as the number of data files increases from frequent small writes. What maintenance operation should they perform?
options: Run Iceberg table compaction to merge small files into larger, optimally sized files
Delete and recreate the Iceberg table
Increase the Athena query timeout
Switch from Iceberg to standard Parquet partitions
questions.explanation: Iceberg table compaction (also called rewrite_data_files) merges many small files into fewer, larger files. This reduces metadata overhead and improves query performance by reducing the number of files that must be opened and read.

questions.question: A company needs to share a Redshift dataset with another AWS account without copying the data. Which feature should they use?
options: Redshift Data Sharing
Redshift Cross-Region Snapshots
Redshift COPY command from S3
AWS Glue ETL job
questions.explanation: Redshift Data Sharing allows you to share live data across Redshift clusters and AWS accounts without physically copying or moving data. Consumer clusters can query the shared data in real-time while the producer maintains a single source of truth.

questions.question: Which service acts as the Apache Hive metastore equivalent in the AWS ecosystem, providing table metadata for Athena, EMR, and Redshift Spectrum?
options: AWS Glue Data Catalog
Amazon DynamoDB
AWS Systems Manager Parameter Store
Amazon RDS
questions.explanation: The AWS Glue Data Catalog serves as the Hive-compatible metastore for the AWS analytics ecosystem. Athena, EMR, Redshift Spectrum, and Glue ETL all use it to discover table schemas, partition information, and data locations.

questions.question: A data engineer needs to organize a multi-tenant data lake where each tenant's data must be logically separated but queryable together for aggregate analytics. Which approach is BEST?
options: Use a single S3 bucket with prefix-based partitioning by tenant_id and enforce access via IAM and Lake Formation
Create a separate S3 bucket for each tenant
Store all tenant data in a single unpartitioned file
Use separate AWS accounts for each tenant
questions.explanation: Using a single bucket with prefix-based partitioning by tenant_id enables efficient cross-tenant analytics while maintaining logical separation. Lake Formation provides fine-grained column-level and row-level access controls to enforce tenant isolation.

questions.question: A Redshift data warehouse needs to perform a bulk load of 500 GB of data from S3. What is the MOST efficient approach?
options: Use the COPY command with multiple compressed files sized to match the number of slices in the cluster
Use individual INSERT statements from a Glue ETL job
Use the UNLOAD command followed by COPY
Use a single large uncompressed file with the COPY command
questions.explanation: The Redshift COPY command is optimized for parallel bulk loading. Using multiple compressed files (ideally matching or being a multiple of the number of cluster slices) allows Redshift to load data in parallel across all slices, maximizing throughput.

questions.question: A data engineer is designing a data catalog strategy. The data lake has tables in S3 discovered by Glue Crawlers, tables in Redshift, and data in RDS. How should they provide a unified catalog?
options: Use the Glue Data Catalog as the central metadata repository with Glue connections for Redshift and RDS, and crawlers for S3
Create separate metadata stores for each data source
Use Amazon Athena as the unified catalog
Build a custom metadata database in DynamoDB
questions.explanation: The Glue Data Catalog serves as a unified metadata repository. Glue Crawlers discover S3 schemas, Glue connections catalog Redshift and RDS tables, and all are queryable from Athena, EMR, and Redshift Spectrum through the same catalog.

questions.question: Which S3 storage class offers the lowest cost for data that is accessed less than once a year and has a retrieval time of up to 12 hours?
options: S3 Glacier Deep Archive
S3 Glacier Flexible Retrieval
S3 Standard-IA
S3 One Zone-IA
questions.explanation: S3 Glacier Deep Archive is the lowest-cost S3 storage class, designed for data that is retained for 7-10 years and accessed once or twice per year. Standard retrieval takes up to 12 hours, with bulk retrieval taking up to 48 hours.

questions.question: What is the primary benefit of using Redshift Materialized Views?
options: They pre-compute and store query results, enabling faster subsequent queries on complex aggregations
They create virtual tables without storing data
They automatically scale the Redshift cluster
They enable cross-region replication of Redshift data
questions.explanation: Redshift Materialized Views store pre-computed query results physically. When users query the materialized view, Redshift can return results directly from the stored data instead of re-executing the complex query, significantly improving performance for repeated analytical queries.

questions.question: A data engineer needs to choose between Apache Iceberg and Apache Hudi for a data lake. The primary requirements are CDC (change data capture) ingestion from databases and record-level upserts. Which format is BETTER suited and why?
options: Apache Hudi, because it was specifically designed for CDC use cases and provides optimized record-level upserts with Merge-on-Read tables
Apache Iceberg, because it provides better query performance
Both are identical for CDC workloads
Neither supports record-level operations
questions.explanation: Apache Hudi (Hadoop Upserts Deletes and Incrementals) was specifically designed for CDC and incremental data processing. Its Merge-on-Read table type is optimized for write-heavy workloads with frequent upserts, making it the better choice for CDC ingestion patterns.

questions.question: A Redshift cluster is experiencing high disk usage. The data engineer discovers that deleted rows are consuming significant space. What should they do?
options: Run VACUUM DELETE to reclaim space from deleted rows, followed by ANALYZE to update statistics
Resize the cluster to add more storage
Drop and recreate the affected tables
Increase the Redshift snapshot retention period
questions.explanation: Redshift uses a soft-delete model where deleted rows are marked but not immediately removed. VACUUM DELETE reclaims the space used by these deleted rows. ANALYZE should follow to update table statistics for the query optimizer.

questions.question: A Glue Crawler discovers a new column in the source data that was not in the original table schema. How should the crawler handle this?
options: Configure the crawler's schema change policy to add new columns while keeping existing ones
Delete the existing table and let the crawler recreate it
Manually add the column to the Data Catalog
Ignore the new column by setting the crawler to read-only mode
questions.explanation: Glue Crawlers have configurable schema change policies. Setting the policy to 'Add new columns only' allows the crawler to append new columns to the existing table definition without modifying or removing existing columns, preventing breaking changes.

questions.question: Which AWS service provides centralized governance for a data lake, including fine-grained access control at the database, table, column, and row level?
options: AWS Lake Formation
AWS Glue Data Catalog
Amazon Macie
AWS IAM
questions.explanation: AWS Lake Formation provides centralized governance for data lakes with fine-grained access controls. It offers column-level and row-level security, tag-based access control, and cross-account sharing, all managed from a central permissions model.

questions.question: What type of key does Redshift use to physically sort data within each slice to enable efficient range-restricted scans?
options: Sort key
Distribution key
Primary key
Foreign key
questions.explanation: Redshift sort keys determine the physical order of data on disk within each slice. When queries filter on sort key columns, Redshift uses zone maps to skip blocks that don't contain matching data, dramatically reducing I/O.

questions.question: A company's S3 data lake has grown to 500 TB. They need to optimize storage costs without impacting query performance for recent data. Objects older than 6 months are queried unpredictably. Which strategy is BEST?
options: Use S3 Intelligent-Tiering for objects older than 6 months, keeping recent data in S3 Standard
Move all data to S3 Glacier after 6 months
Keep all data in S3 Standard
Use S3 One Zone-IA for all data
questions.explanation: S3 Intelligent-Tiering is ideal for data with unpredictable access patterns. It automatically moves objects between frequent and infrequent access tiers without retrieval fees or performance impact. Keeping recent data in Standard ensures consistent performance for active workloads.

questions.question: A data engineer needs to implement data lineage tracking across their data lake. They want to track which Glue ETL jobs produced which tables and how data flows from source to destination. Which approach is MOST comprehensive?
options: Use AWS Glue Data Catalog with Glue job metadata, combined with Amazon DataZone for cataloging and lineage visualization
Manually maintain a spreadsheet of data flows
Use CloudTrail logs to track S3 access patterns
Create custom tags on S3 objects
questions.explanation: Amazon DataZone provides data cataloging, governance, and lineage tracking capabilities. Combined with Glue Data Catalog metadata and Glue job information, it creates a comprehensive data lineage view showing how data flows through ETL pipelines.

questions.question: A data warehouse team wants to run Redshift queries that combine local Redshift data with data in S3 in a single query. Which combination of features enables this?
options: Redshift Spectrum with external tables pointing to S3 data registered in the Glue Data Catalog
Redshift COPY command to load S3 data first
Redshift Federated Query to Amazon S3
Redshift Data Sharing with an S3 endpoint
questions.explanation: Redshift Spectrum enables querying S3 data through external tables defined in the Glue Data Catalog. These external tables can be joined with local Redshift tables in a single SQL query, providing a unified view across the data warehouse and data lake.

questions.question: A data engineer needs to store semi-structured data (JSON documents) with single-digit millisecond latency for key-value lookups. Which AWS service is BEST suited?
options: Amazon DynamoDB
Amazon S3
Amazon Redshift
Amazon RDS PostgreSQL
questions.explanation: Amazon DynamoDB is a fully managed NoSQL database that provides single-digit millisecond performance for key-value and document data. It scales automatically and is ideal for high-throughput, low-latency access patterns.

questions.question: A data engineer discovers that the Glue Data Catalog has stale partition metadata for an S3 table because new partitions are added by an external process. How should they keep the catalog in sync?
options: Schedule a Glue Crawler to run periodically, or use MSCK REPAIR TABLE in Athena after new partitions are added
Manually add partitions through the AWS Console
Delete and recreate the table daily
Disable partitioning entirely
questions.explanation: A scheduled Glue Crawler will discover new partitions and update the Data Catalog. Alternatively, running MSCK REPAIR TABLE in Athena scans for new Hive-compatible partitions in S3 and adds them to the catalog. For automation, the Glue Partition API (batch_create_partition) can be called directly.

questions.question: Redshift Serverless is being considered as an alternative to a provisioned cluster. Which scenario is the BEST fit for Redshift Serverless?
options: Variable workloads with unpredictable query patterns and teams that want to avoid capacity planning
Steady-state workloads running 24/7 with predictable capacity needs
Workloads that require Reserved Instance pricing
Clusters that need specific node types for compliance
questions.explanation: Redshift Serverless is ideal for variable workloads where capacity needs are unpredictable. It automatically provisions and scales compute resources, charges based on usage (RPUs), and eliminates the need for cluster management and capacity planning.

questions.question: What is the MINIMUM object size recommended for transitioning objects from S3 Standard to S3 Standard-IA to be cost-effective?
options: 128 KB
1 KB
1 MB
5 GB
questions.explanation: S3 Standard-IA has a minimum billable object size of 128 KB. Objects smaller than 128 KB are charged as if they were 128 KB, making the transition cost-ineffective for small objects. Lifecycle policies should account for this minimum size.

questions.question: A Redshift user runs a complex query that takes 30 minutes. The same query will run daily with updated data. How can the data engineer speed up subsequent runs?
options: Create a Materialized View and set it to auto-refresh
Increase the WLM query timeout
Disable result caching
Add more Redshift users to distribute the load
questions.explanation: A Materialized View pre-computes and stores the query results. With auto-refresh enabled, Redshift automatically updates the materialized view when the underlying data changes, providing fast query responses for subsequent runs.

questions.question: A data engineer is building a lakehouse architecture. They need to support both batch analytics and real-time point lookups on the same dataset. Which combination is MOST appropriate?
options: S3 data lake with Apache Iceberg for batch analytics, and DynamoDB for real-time point lookups, synchronized via a streaming pipeline
Store everything in Redshift and use Redshift Serverless for real-time queries
Use only DynamoDB for both batch analytics and point lookups
Use only S3 with Athena for both workloads
questions.explanation: A lakehouse architecture can combine S3 with Iceberg for efficient batch analytics (Athena, EMR) and DynamoDB for single-digit millisecond point lookups. A streaming pipeline (e.g., Lambda processing DynamoDB Streams) keeps both stores synchronized.

questions.question: Which Glue Crawler configuration determines what happens when the crawler detects that a table's schema has changed?
options: Schema change policy
Crawler schedule
Classifier priority
Recrawl policy
questions.explanation: The schema change policy controls how the crawler handles schema differences. Options include updating the table definition in place, adding new columns only, or logging the change without updating. This protects downstream consumers from unexpected schema changes.

questions.question: A Redshift cluster uses Workload Management (WLM) with a single default queue. During peak hours, short dashboard queries are stuck behind long-running ETL queries. How should the data engineer configure WLM?
options: Create separate WLM queues: a high-priority queue for short dashboard queries with query monitoring rules, and a lower-priority queue for ETL
Increase the cluster size to handle all queries simultaneously
Disable WLM entirely
Schedule ETL queries to run only off-peak hours
questions.explanation: Creating separate WLM queues with different concurrency levels and priorities ensures that short dashboard queries get dedicated resources and are not blocked by long-running ETL. Query monitoring rules can automatically route or abort queries that exceed thresholds.

questions.question: S3 Object Lock provides which of the following capabilities?
options: WORM (Write Once Read Many) protection that prevents objects from being deleted or overwritten for a specified retention period
Encryption of objects at rest using customer-managed keys
Replication of objects to another region
Automatic tier transitions based on access patterns
questions.explanation: S3 Object Lock provides WORM protection, preventing objects from being deleted or overwritten for a defined retention period. It supports Governance mode (admin override) and Compliance mode (no one can override, including root), ensuring regulatory compliance.

questions.question: A data lake has tables cataloged in Glue that are consumed by Athena, EMR, and Redshift Spectrum. The data engineer needs to ensure all three services see consistent table definitions. How should they manage this?
options: Use the Glue Data Catalog as the single source of truth with Lake Formation permissions controlling access for all three services
Maintain separate metadata stores for each service
Replicate the Glue Data Catalog across regions
Use Athena views to abstract the underlying tables
questions.explanation: The Glue Data Catalog serves as the unified Hive metastore for Athena, EMR, and Redshift Spectrum. All three services read from the same catalog, ensuring consistent table definitions. Lake Formation provides centralized access control across all consumers.

questions.question: Which S3 feature allows you to retrieve a subset of data from an object using SQL expressions, reducing the amount of data transferred?
options: S3 Select
S3 Transfer Acceleration
S3 Batch Operations
S3 Access Points
questions.explanation: S3 Select allows you to use SQL expressions to retrieve only the subset of data you need from an S3 object. This reduces data transfer and processing time by filtering data at the storage layer instead of downloading the entire object.

questions.question: Which AWS service orchestrates multi-step data pipelines using visual workflows with built-in error handling and retry logic?
options: AWS Step Functions
Amazon EventBridge
AWS Glue Triggers
Amazon SQS
questions.explanation: AWS Step Functions is a serverless orchestration service that lets you build visual workflows (state machines) to coordinate multiple AWS services. It provides built-in error handling, retries, parallel execution, and conditional branching for data pipelines.

questions.question: What is Amazon MWAA (Managed Workflows for Apache Airflow) used for?
options: Orchestrating complex data pipelines using Apache Airflow DAGs in a managed environment
Streaming data ingestion from IoT devices
Real-time data transformation
Machine learning model training
questions.explanation: Amazon MWAA provides a fully managed Apache Airflow environment for authoring, scheduling, and monitoring data pipelines defined as Directed Acyclic Graphs (DAGs). It handles the infrastructure, scaling, and availability of the Airflow components.

questions.question: Which AWS service enables you to schedule events on a cron or rate-based schedule and trigger targets like Lambda, Step Functions, or Glue jobs?
options: Amazon EventBridge
Amazon CloudWatch Alarms
AWS Config
Amazon SQS
questions.explanation: Amazon EventBridge (formerly CloudWatch Events) supports cron and rate-based scheduling rules that can trigger over 20 AWS service targets including Lambda, Step Functions, Glue jobs, and ECS tasks. It is the recommended service for event-driven pipeline scheduling.

questions.question: A data pipeline must run three Glue ETL jobs in sequence: extract, transform, then load. If any job fails, the pipeline should stop and send a notification. Which orchestration approach is BEST?
options: AWS Step Functions with sequential states for each Glue job, a Catch block for errors, and an SNS notification on failure
Three separate EventBridge cron schedules with staggered times
Glue Workflows with triggers chained sequentially
A Lambda function that starts each Glue job and polls for completion
questions.explanation: Step Functions provides native error handling with Catch and Retry blocks, sequential state execution, and built-in integration with Glue and SNS. This gives complete visibility into pipeline state, automatic error handling, and notification without custom polling code.

questions.question: A data engineer is migrating Airflow DAGs from an on-premises server to AWS. The DAGs use custom Python packages and connect to S3, Redshift, and RDS. Which AWS service should they use?
options: Amazon MWAA with custom plugins and requirements.txt
AWS Step Functions
Amazon ECS with a custom Airflow container
AWS Batch
questions.explanation: Amazon MWAA supports custom plugins (plugins.zip) and Python packages (requirements.txt) that can be uploaded to S3. This allows migration of existing DAGs with their dependencies. MWAA also provides managed connections to S3, Redshift, and RDS.

questions.question: Which AWS service provides metrics, dashboards, alarms, and logs for monitoring data pipeline health?
options: Amazon CloudWatch
AWS CloudTrail
Amazon Inspector
AWS Config
questions.explanation: Amazon CloudWatch provides a unified monitoring solution with custom metrics, dashboards, alarms, and log aggregation. It integrates with Glue, EMR, Lambda, Kinesis, and other AWS services to provide comprehensive pipeline monitoring.

questions.question: A data engineer wants to be alerted when a Glue ETL job fails. Which CloudWatch feature should they configure?
options: A CloudWatch Alarm on the glue.driver.aggregate.numFailedTasks metric with an SNS notification action
A CloudWatch Dashboard showing Glue job history
CloudWatch Logs Insights queries
CloudWatch Container Insights
questions.explanation: CloudWatch Alarms can monitor Glue job metrics like numFailedTasks. When the metric exceeds the threshold (indicating a failure), the alarm triggers an SNS notification to alert the engineering team via email, Slack, or PagerDuty.

questions.question: How can a data engineer identify the root cause of a slow Spark job running on EMR?
options: Use the Spark UI to analyze stage execution times, task distribution, and shuffle metrics
Increase the number of EMR core nodes and re-run
Check CloudTrail for API call latency
Review S3 access logs
questions.explanation: The Spark UI (accessible via the EMR console) shows detailed execution information including DAG visualization, stage execution times, task-level metrics, shuffle read/write sizes, and memory usage. This helps pinpoint bottlenecks like data skew or excessive shuffling.

questions.question: What is schema evolution in the context of data pipelines?
options: The ability to accommodate changes in data structure (added/removed/modified columns) over time without breaking existing pipelines
Converting data from one format to another
Versioning of database schemas using migration scripts
Encrypting schema metadata in the data catalog
questions.explanation: Schema evolution refers to the ability of a data system to handle changes to the data schema over time, such as adding new columns, removing fields, or changing data types, without requiring all consumers and pipelines to be updated simultaneously.

questions.question: A data pipeline receives data where a column's type changed from integer to string in recent source files. The downstream Athena table expects an integer. How should the data engineer handle this?
options: Use a Glue ETL job with DynamicFrame ResolveChoice to cast the column to the expected type and route incompatible records to an error path
Change the Athena table schema to string
Delete the incompatible files from S3
Ignore the type mismatch and let Athena return nulls
questions.explanation: Glue DynamicFrame's ResolveChoice transformation handles type ambiguities by letting you cast to the expected type, keep both types, or route records to different paths. This preserves data quality while preventing pipeline failures from schema drift.

questions.question: A data engineer needs to implement data validation rules that check for null values, uniqueness constraints, and value ranges during ETL processing. Which AWS service provides this natively?
options: AWS Glue Data Quality with DQDL rules
Amazon Athena with CHECK constraints
AWS Lambda with custom validation code
Amazon DynamoDB conditional writes
questions.explanation: AWS Glue Data Quality uses the Data Quality Definition Language (DQDL) to define validation rules like IsComplete, IsUnique, ColumnValues, and CustomSql. These rules run within Glue ETL jobs and can route failing records to a quarantine path.

questions.question: A complex data pipeline has 15 interdependent Glue jobs, Lambda functions, and EMR steps. The data engineer needs observability into the entire pipeline execution including timing, dependencies, and failures. Which orchestration service provides the BEST visibility?
options: AWS Step Functions with its visual execution graph showing the state of each step in real-time
Amazon MWAA with Airflow's Gantt chart view
Chaining Glue Workflows with triggers
EventBridge rules triggering each step independently
questions.explanation: AWS Step Functions provides a visual execution graph that shows each step's status (running, succeeded, failed) in real-time. For complex pipelines with mixed service types (Glue, Lambda, EMR), Step Functions offers the most comprehensive view of the entire pipeline state.

questions.question: A Kinesis Data Streams consumer application is experiencing increasing IteratorAge (the age of the last record read). What does this indicate and how should it be resolved?
options: The consumer is falling behind the producer; increase consumer parallelism by adding more KCL workers or enabling enhanced fan-out
The stream has too many shards; reduce the shard count
The data retention period is too long; shorten it
The producer is sending data too slowly; increase producer throughput
questions.explanation: Increasing IteratorAge means the consumer is processing records slower than they are being produced. Solutions include adding more KCL worker instances, enabling enhanced fan-out for dedicated per-consumer throughput, or optimizing the consumer's record processing logic.

questions.question: A data engineer discovers that records failing ETL validation are being silently dropped. They need to implement a dead letter queue pattern for failed records. Which approach is BEST for a Glue ETL job?
options: Use Glue Data Quality rules to route failing records to a separate S3 error path, then trigger an SNS notification
Log failed records to CloudWatch Logs
Stop the entire job when any record fails validation
Retry failed records indefinitely
questions.explanation: Routing failing records to a separate S3 path creates a dead letter queue pattern where invalid data is preserved for inspection and reprocessing. Combining this with SNS notifications alerts the team to data quality issues without blocking the pipeline.

questions.question: What is data profiling in the context of data quality?
options: Analyzing data to understand its structure, content, quality, and statistical properties
Encrypting data to protect sensitive information
Converting data between different formats
Compressing data to reduce storage costs
questions.explanation: Data profiling examines data to understand its structure (schemas, types), content (value distributions, patterns), quality (completeness, accuracy), and statistical properties (min, max, mean, cardinality). AWS Glue DataBrew provides built-in data profiling capabilities.

questions.question: Which feature of AWS Step Functions allows multiple branches of a workflow to execute in parallel and wait for all to complete before proceeding?
options: Parallel state
Choice state
Map state
Wait state
questions.explanation: The Parallel state in Step Functions executes multiple branches concurrently. All branches must complete (or fail) before the workflow proceeds to the next state. This is useful for running independent ETL jobs simultaneously to reduce total pipeline duration.

questions.question: A data engineer needs to set up automated alerts when a Glue Crawler detects a schema change in a source table. Which approach works?
options: Configure an EventBridge rule for Glue Crawler state change events and trigger an SNS notification
Check the Glue Data Catalog manually after each crawler run
Enable CloudWatch Container Insights for Glue
Use AWS Config rules to monitor Glue resources
questions.explanation: Glue Crawlers emit events to EventBridge when they complete, including information about schema changes detected. An EventBridge rule can match these events and trigger an SNS notification, Lambda function, or other targets to alert the team.

questions.question: A data pipeline processes financial transactions and must ensure that no records are lost or duplicated end-to-end. Which combination of techniques provides the STRONGEST guarantee?
options: Idempotent writes using unique transaction IDs, Glue Job Bookmarks for incremental reads, and reconciliation counts at each pipeline stage
At-least-once delivery with SQS and manual deduplication
Lambda with event source mapping and default retry behavior
Kinesis Data Firehose with S3 delivery
questions.explanation: Idempotent writes (using unique IDs to prevent duplicates), Job Bookmarks (to prevent reprocessing), and reconciliation counts (to detect missing records) together provide end-to-end exactly-once processing semantics for critical financial data.

questions.question: An Airflow DAG on MWAA needs to trigger an EMR Spark job, wait for it to complete, then run a Redshift COPY command. Which Airflow operators should be used?
options: EmrAddStepsOperator with EmrStepSensor, followed by RedshiftSQLOperator
BashOperator running AWS CLI commands for both steps
PythonOperator with boto3 calls and time.sleep() loops
S3KeySensor followed by a Lambda trigger
questions.explanation: EmrAddStepsOperator submits the Spark step to EMR, EmrStepSensor waits for completion (without blocking the Airflow worker), and RedshiftSQLOperator executes the COPY command. This uses purpose-built operators for clean, maintainable DAGs.

questions.question: A data lake table needs to support adding new columns without breaking existing Athena queries. Which table format feature enables this?
options: Schema evolution with backward compatibility (new columns have default null values)
Immutable schemas that prevent any changes
Full table scans on every query
Automatic schema inference from file headers
questions.explanation: Schema evolution with backward compatibility allows adding new columns to a table definition. Existing queries continue to work because new columns default to null for older data files. Apache Iceberg, Hudi, and Delta Lake all support this pattern.

questions.question: Which AWS service records all API calls made in your AWS account, providing an audit trail of who did what and when?
options: AWS CloudTrail
Amazon CloudWatch
AWS Config
Amazon GuardDuty
questions.explanation: AWS CloudTrail records API calls made in your AWS account, including the identity of the caller, time, source IP, parameters, and response. This provides a complete audit trail for compliance and troubleshooting.

questions.question: A Glue ETL job processes customer records and must reject any record where the email field is null or does not match a valid email pattern. How should this be implemented?
options: Define Glue Data Quality rules: IsComplete 'email' and Matches 'email' with a regex pattern, and route failures to a quarantine path
Use a Filter transformation that silently drops invalid records
Check emails only after loading into Redshift
Use AWS Lambda to validate each record individually
questions.explanation: Glue Data Quality DQDL rules like IsComplete (null check) and Matches (regex validation) enforce data quality during ETL. Routing failures to quarantine preserves invalid records for review while ensuring only clean data proceeds to the destination.

questions.question: Which AWS Step Functions feature allows you to iterate over a collection and run a set of steps for each item?
options: Map state
Parallel state
Choice state
Pass state
questions.explanation: The Map state in Step Functions iterates over an input array and runs a set of steps for each element. It supports both inline mode (up to 40 concurrent iterations) and distributed mode (up to 10,000 parallel child executions) for large-scale processing.

questions.question: An EMR cluster running a critical Spark pipeline occasionally fails due to Spot Instance terminations. The data engineer needs to ensure pipeline resilience. What is the BEST approach?
options: Use on-demand instances for core nodes (HDFS data), Spot for task nodes, and enable Spark checkpointing with S3 as the checkpoint location
Use only on-demand instances for all node types
Increase the Spot bid price to the on-demand price
Disable HDFS and write all intermediate data to S3
questions.explanation: Core nodes store HDFS data and should use on-demand instances for stability. Task nodes only run compute and can tolerate Spot interruptions. Spark checkpointing to S3 ensures that lost task work can be recovered from the last checkpoint instead of restarting from scratch.

questions.question: A streaming pipeline consumes from Kinesis Data Streams and writes to an Iceberg table on S3. Occasionally, late-arriving data needs to be merged with previously written partitions. How should the data engineer handle this?
options: Use Iceberg's MERGE INTO statement to upsert late-arriving records into existing partitions based on a primary key
Overwrite entire partitions whenever late data arrives
Drop late-arriving data to maintain partition consistency
Store late data in a separate table and join at query time
questions.explanation: Apache Iceberg's MERGE INTO operation performs atomic upserts — inserting new records and updating existing ones based on a primary key match. This handles late-arriving data without overwriting entire partitions or requiring separate tables.

questions.question: Which AWS service can profile datasets and suggest over 250 built-in transformations through a visual interface without writing code?
options: AWS Glue DataBrew
AWS Glue ETL
Amazon QuickSight
Amazon SageMaker Data Wrangler
questions.explanation: AWS Glue DataBrew provides visual data profiling that automatically generates statistics, distributions, and quality assessments. It suggests relevant transformations from its library of 250+ built-in transforms, enabling no-code data preparation.

questions.question: A data engineer needs to set up monitoring for a data pipeline that runs on a schedule. They want to detect when the pipeline does NOT run (e.g., the scheduler fails silently). Which approach is BEST?
options: Create a CloudWatch Alarm with a MISSING data treatment that triggers when the expected pipeline completion metric is not received within the expected window
Check the pipeline status manually each morning
Set up a CloudWatch Dashboard and review it periodically
Enable AWS Config rules for the pipeline resources
questions.explanation: CloudWatch Alarms with 'treat missing data as breaching' (MISSING data treatment) will trigger when the expected metric (e.g., pipeline completion) is not received within the evaluation period. This detects silent failures where the pipeline never starts.

questions.question: A data pipeline must process 1,000 independent files in parallel, with each file requiring a Glue ETL job. The pipeline must handle up to 50 concurrent Glue jobs. Which Step Functions approach scales BEST?
options: Distributed Map state with a MaxConcurrency of 50, processing the S3 file list as input
A Parallel state with 1,000 branches
A single Glue job that processes all 1,000 files sequentially
1,000 separate Step Functions executions triggered by EventBridge
questions.explanation: The Step Functions Distributed Map state can process large-scale workloads by iterating over an S3 manifest or input array. MaxConcurrency controls how many child executions run simultaneously (50 in this case), and it can handle up to 10,000 parallel child executions.

questions.question: Which metric should a data engineer track to measure the freshness of data in a data warehouse?
options: Data latency — the time between when data is generated at the source and when it becomes available for queries
Query execution time
Storage utilization
Number of tables in the data catalog
questions.explanation: Data latency (or data freshness) measures the gap between event time and availability time. Tracking this metric ensures that the data warehouse serves sufficiently current data for business decisions and SLAs are met.

questions.question: A data engineer receives an error that a Glue ETL job cannot write to a Redshift table because a new column exists in the source that does not exist in the target. What is the BEST automated solution?
options: Use Glue's DynamicFrame ApplyMapping to select only the columns that match the target schema, and log the unmapped columns for review
Manually alter the Redshift table to add the new column
Delete the source data that contains the new column
Disable schema validation in the Glue job
questions.explanation: ApplyMapping in DynamicFrame lets you explicitly map source columns to target columns, effectively ignoring new source columns that don't have a target mapping. Logging unmapped columns alerts the team to schema drift for future review.

questions.question: A Glue Streaming ETL job processing from Kinesis Data Streams has a checkpoint interval of 60 seconds. If the job fails and restarts, what is the maximum amount of data that might be reprocessed?
options: Up to 60 seconds of data since the last successful checkpoint
All data from the beginning of the stream
No data — Glue Streaming jobs never reprocess
Up to 24 hours of data
questions.explanation: Glue Streaming ETL jobs checkpoint their position in the Kinesis stream at the configured interval. On restart, the job resumes from the last checkpoint, meaning up to one checkpoint interval of data may be reprocessed. This makes downstream writes need to be idempotent.

questions.question: A data warehouse receives data from 20 different source systems. The data engineer needs to implement automated data quality monitoring that tracks completeness, freshness, and schema conformity across all sources. Which comprehensive approach is BEST?
options: Implement Glue Data Quality rules in each ETL job, publish quality metrics to CloudWatch, and create a unified dashboard with alarms for SLA breaches
Spot-check data manually each week
Rely on downstream users to report data issues
Run Athena queries to count rows in each table daily
questions.explanation: Embedding Glue Data Quality rules in each ETL job creates automated quality gates. Publishing results to CloudWatch enables centralized monitoring, dashboards provide visibility, and alarms ensure that SLA breaches are detected and addressed promptly.

questions.question: Which Glue Workflow feature automatically starts a downstream Glue job when an upstream crawl or job completes successfully?
options: Glue Triggers (conditional triggers based on job/crawler completion events)
Glue Job Bookmarks
Glue Classifiers
Glue Connections
questions.explanation: Glue Triggers can be configured as conditional triggers that fire when a specific Glue job or crawler completes (succeeds, fails, or times out). These can be chained together in a Glue Workflow to create multi-step pipelines with automated dependencies.

questions.question: Which AWS service provides centralized access management for data lakes, allowing administrators to grant and revoke permissions at the database, table, and column level?
options: AWS Lake Formation
AWS IAM
Amazon Macie
AWS KMS
questions.explanation: AWS Lake Formation provides centralized, fine-grained access control for data lakes. Administrators can grant permissions at the database, table, column, and row level using a centralized console, replacing the complex combination of IAM policies and S3 bucket policies.

questions.question: Which IAM feature allows you to grant temporary security credentials to users or services to access AWS resources?
options: IAM Roles with temporary credentials via STS (Security Token Service)
IAM Access Keys
IAM User passwords
IAM Groups
questions.explanation: IAM Roles provide temporary security credentials via AWS STS (Security Token Service). This is more secure than long-term access keys because credentials automatically expire and cannot be shared or leaked permanently.

questions.question: Which AWS service provides managed encryption key creation, storage, and management for encrypting data at rest?
options: AWS Key Management Service (KMS)
AWS Certificate Manager
AWS Secrets Manager
AWS CloudHSM
questions.explanation: AWS KMS is a managed service for creating and controlling encryption keys used to encrypt data across AWS services. It integrates natively with S3, Redshift, Glue, RDS, and many other services for encryption at rest.

questions.question: What is the difference between SSE-S3, SSE-KMS, and SSE-C encryption for S3?
options: SSE-S3 uses S3-managed keys, SSE-KMS uses customer-managed KMS keys with audit trails, SSE-C uses customer-provided keys
They all use the same encryption but with different pricing
SSE-S3 is for small objects, SSE-KMS for medium, SSE-C for large
SSE-S3 encrypts in transit, SSE-KMS at rest, SSE-C both
questions.explanation: SSE-S3 uses Amazon-managed keys (simplest), SSE-KMS uses AWS KMS keys (provides audit trails via CloudTrail and key rotation), and SSE-C requires you to manage and provide encryption keys with each request. SSE-KMS is recommended for most data engineering workloads.

questions.question: A data engineer needs to restrict access to specific columns in a Glue Data Catalog table so that analysts cannot see PII columns (SSN, email). Which service provides this capability?
options: AWS Lake Formation column-level permissions
S3 bucket policies
IAM policies with S3 prefix conditions
Glue Data Catalog resource policies
questions.explanation: AWS Lake Formation supports column-level permissions, allowing administrators to grant or revoke access to specific columns in a Data Catalog table. When analysts query through Athena or Redshift Spectrum, Lake Formation enforces these permissions and hides restricted columns.

questions.question: A company requires that all data in their Redshift cluster be encrypted at rest using a customer-managed encryption key. How should this be configured?
options: Enable Redshift cluster encryption with an AWS KMS customer-managed key (CMK)
Encrypt data in the ETL pipeline before loading into Redshift
Use SSL connections to Redshift
Enable S3 encryption for Redshift snapshots only
